revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Gongen]][[es:Gongen]][[fa:گونگن]][[fi:Gongen]][[fr:Gongen]][[it:Gongen]][[ja:権現]][[ko:곤겐]][[zh:權現]]
{{Short description|Incarnations of the buddha as kami in ancient Japanese shinto-buddhism}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qid=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} enshrines {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a {{ill|Buddhahood|en|Buddhahood|qid=Q56247062|lt=buddha|1=Buddhahood}} in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to {{ill|Salvation|en|Salvation|qid=Q192054|lt=Salvation|1=Salvation}}, during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are {{ill|Synonym|en|Synonym|qid=Q42106|lt=Synonym|1=Synonym}}s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|qid=Q48362|lt=Mahayana Buddhist|1=Mahayana}} notion of {{ill|Upaya|en|Upaya|qid=Q581473|lt=Upaya|1=Upaya}}, "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qid=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|qid=Q320229|lt=Emperor Go-Mizunoo|1=Emperor Go-Mizunoo}})]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and {{ill|Ethnic religion|en|Ethnic religion|qid=Q1189816|lt=indigenous religious practice|1=Ethnic religion}} in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "{{ill|Syncretism|en|Syncretism|qid=Q172904|lt=Syncretism|1=Syncretism}} of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai Buddhism|1=Tendai}} and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to {{ill|Mount Iwaki|en|Mount Iwaki|qid=Q713857|lt=Mount Iwaki|1=Mount Iwaki}}, a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q193849|lt=Avalokiteśvara|1=Avalokiteśvara}} ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Kannon|Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Bhaisajyaguru|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many {{ill|Mount Haku|en|Mount Haku|qid=Q2639098|lt=Mount Haku|1=Mount Haku}} shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[:en:Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|qid=Q742360|lt=Tengu|1=Tengu}} and represents the kami of {{ill|Mount Iizuna|en|Mount Iizuna|qid=Q11666652|lt=Mount Iizuna|1=Mount Iizuna}}.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現|1=Izusan Gongen}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|qid=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, enshrined in the [[Izusan Shrine|Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} spread from {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|qid=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}}. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|qid=Q10935105|lt=Oyamakui no Kami|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}. * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in {{ill|Jingo-ji|en|Jingo-ji|qid=Q1075034|lt=Jingo-ji|1=Jingo-ji}} in Takao as the tutelary kami of {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}} by {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|qid=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}}.<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} posthumously enshrined in so-called {{ill|Tōshō-gū|en|Tōshō-gū|qid=Q1190456|lt=Tōshō-gū|1=Tōshō-gū}} shrines present all over Japan. The original one is {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qid=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} in {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|qid=Q235753|lt=Nikkō|1=Nikkō}}. * Ishizuchi Kongo Zaō Dai Gongen (石鎚金剛蔵王大権現) one of two Zaō Gongen manifestations in Japan. The trio of Mount Ishizuchi is a manifestaion of [[Amitābha]], {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q193849|lt=Avalokiteśvara|1=Avalokiteśvara}}, and {{ill|Mahasthamaprapta|en|Mahasthamaprapta|qid=Q1465683|lt=Mahasthamaprapta.|1=Mahasthamaprapta}} * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} one of two Zaō Gongen manifestations in Japan. The trio from the Omine mountain range is a manifestation of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Thousand-armed Kannon Avalokitesvara, and Maitreya.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}.</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}.<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture
==Notes==
{{Reflist|2}}
== References == *{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism — A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo|year=2000 |edition=First |pages=232 pages |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |language= }} * {{cite book | last = [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] | first = [[Mark Teeuwen]] (editors) | title = Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | date = July 2000 | location = Honolulu | language = | isbn = 978-0-8248-2363-4 | oclc = 43487317 }}
{{Commons category|Gongen}}
